title: Robinhood Tripled Revenue, Turned a $1B Loss Into $1.9B Profit, and the Stock Is Down 35%. Make It Make Sense.
subtitle: From meme-stock punchline to $4.47B revenue machine with software margins. $5.66B in net cash, zero debt, prediction markets, a credit card, AI agents. Earnings in 10 days. And the stock sits near 52-week lows.
summary: Robinhood Markets (HOOD) has executed one of the most dramatic turnarounds in fintech history — revenue tripling from $1.36B to $4.47B in three years, net income swinging from a $1.03B loss to $1.88B in profit, with 92.2% gross margins and zero debt. The stock sits near 52-week lows, down 35% from highs, with earnings due July 29.

image.caption: Robinhood has transformed from meme-stock platform to profitable fintech powerhouse.

bodyHtml:
Let's talk about Robinhood. Not the Robinhood of 2022 — the one that was bleeding a billion dollars a year, getting sued by the SEC, and serving as the punchline for every Wall Street guy who wanted to feel superior about retail traders. That company is gone. The company that replaced it just posted $4.47 billion in revenue, $1.88 billion in net income, and 92.2% gross margins. That's software-company margins. The stock is down 35% from its 52-week high. Something is deeply mispriced here.

Let's start with the numbers, because they're almost unbelievable for a company that was written off as a meme-stock casino three years ago. Revenue in FY2025 hit $4.47 billion — up 51.6% year-over-year. That's not a bounce off a pandemic spike. That's sustained, compounding growth. Look at the trajectory: $1.36 billion in 2022. Then $1.87 billion. Then $2.95 billion. Then $4.47 billion. Revenue tripled in three years. No gimmicks. No crypto-bubble sugar highs. Just relentless execution.

The bottom line is the real jaw-dropper. Net income hit $1.88 billion in FY2025. In 2022, the company lost $1.03 billion. That's a swing of nearly $3 billion in three years. Free cash flow sits at $1.58 billion. The balance sheet holds $5.66 billion in net cash — and zero debt. Zero. While most fintechs are burning investor capital or loading up on leverage to chase growth, Robinhood is sitting on a pile of cash the size of a small country's GDP.

This isn't a story about crypto volatility saving the day, either. Yes, crypto trading drove a lot of the early volume. But Robinhood has methodically diversified its revenue base in ways that most investors haven't fully absorbed. The credit card is live. The retirement accounts business is scaling. Prediction markets — the fastest-growing category in finance right now — are gaining traction on the platform. The company has been quietly building AI agents for retail traders: automated portfolio management, tax-loss harvesting, options analysis. These are subscription-grade products with recurring revenue characteristics, not transactional volume that disappears when a bear market hits.

Operating margin is 38.5%. Let that number sit for a moment. A consumer fintech platform with nearly 40% operating margins, $4.5 billion in revenue, and almost no debt — that's not a brokerage. That's a software company. That's the kind of margin profile that gets you a premium multiple in any normal market. But Robinhood isn't getting a premium multiple. The stock trades at 48.5 times trailing earnings — which sounds expensive until you realize the forward P/E is 32.5 times, reflecting analyst expectations for continued growth. Twenty-six analysts cover the stock. The consensus is Buy. The average price target is $118.28, implying 18.3% upside from here.

The stock is near its current price as of this writing. The 52-week range is $63.52 to $153.86. That's a 58% peak-to-trough swing in a year — which makes sense for a stock with a beta of 2.34. This thing moves. When markets sell off, Robinhood gets hit harder. When they rally, it catches fire. The YTD performance of negative 13.2% reflects macro rotation out of high-beta names, not a deterioration in the underlying business. If anything, the business is stronger today than it was when the stock hit $153.

CEO Vladimir Tenev runs a lean operation for a company processing this much volume. Just 2,900 employees. That's fewer people than the IT department of a traditional bank. The margins aren't an accident — they're a deliberate machine built to scale without adding headcount.

The next big catalyst is July 29 — Q2 2026 earnings. The whisper numbers are optimistic. Transaction revenue continues to run hot. The prediction market business is accelerating. And retail traders — who have been sitting on the sidelines for much of the year — are starting to reengage as crypto finds a floor and the equity market digests the AI selloff. If Robinhood delivers a beat and raises guidance, you could see a 20%-plus move in a single session. That's the nature of a 2.34-beta stock with a huge short base and a narrative that's still stuck in 2022.

Here's the bottom line: Robinhood executed arguably the most dramatic turnaround in fintech history. Revenue tripled. Losses turned into billions in profit. A balance sheet went from precarious to pristine. Margins went from zero to software-grade. The stock is down 35% from its high anyway. At some point, the narrative catches up to the numbers. July 29 might be that point.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in HOOD. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
